item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slight necro, but I've got to say this.

It's not that this is an Infinite D-Class Generator. It's more of a Crash Test Dummy creator. The advantage of the D's is they can actually think. They can make a snap decision. They can give the feedback researchers need. You can't put the clones in front of a painting and have them describe it to you. They are really only good for some kinds of medical testing, survival/safety testing, or 'meat grinder' testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same, i was thinking about portal since the gel thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic. A nonstop clean no-drawback source of D class personnel and the Foundation has no interest of doing anything with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, the last note was actually creepy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this article was extremely unnerving, good job azzleflux. Take my +1s, take them all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not wildly enthusiastic, but this gets a +1.
This seems like the ideal SCP for a cautionary tale of cross testing. I mean, they are emotionally unsettling (in the extreme), but most people would have little moral trouble with non-sentient by-design human clones for life-saving research. It is what they were made for.
But that's forgetting that if they emerged from 1610-2 and dissolve back into 1610-2, they are basically 1610-2 formed into an incredibly perfect copy of a human. It doesn't get much more anomalous than that. Using them as SCP crash test dummies is literally smashing 2 SCPs together!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, and it seemed totally in character, to me, for a company to make sort-of-human clones for testing. When you create as much stuff as PL do, they need to do a lot of testing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would be a great idea, except the Foundation tries not cross-test. Putting two anomalous items together (such as these Z-Subjects and whatever SCP they would be used to test) is usually just a bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's rather confusing to me is that the Foundation doesn't use this. Yes, it's horrifying, but it seems like the Foundation is a lot less likely to care about the ethics of using Z-Subjects.

There are a number of means which the Foundation could use to make this less traumatic: just use people that those working with don't personally know, for one. The foundation's D-Class allows people to rationalize that they're doing evil things to evil people.

But make them wear a mask to hide their human face and use people the staff don't know and I could see the foundation all over it.

item.title: SCP-1610
item.content:encoded:
I like the change about the process comatosing the original for only 30 minutes instead of killing them. Definitely feel it is more disturbing if the person lives after the process and we see them getting killed time and time again. Also makes the whole "bring your daughter to work day" more believable. The tone for that part seemed really off for how tragic that would be, but the idea of no one being willing to kill/test on clones of Dr. Greg's daughter is better. I can now actually imagine them letting one of the girls into the vat, having volunteered for the process so they can all see what their daddy's do at work. What exactly they did to show them what else they do at work I'm not sure.

Anyways, I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this, even if the things feel a bit too much like non-remotely-controlled versions of the Gangers from Doctor Who.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If -c and -d aren't intentional references to Cave Johnson and Caroline, it's one hell of a coincidence.

Prometheus in this shows a remarkable lack of understanding of human nature. This project, no matter how it was executed, could never be anything but a horror show. Mass produced human analogs, destined only for destructive testing and eventual dissolution? How did they think people would react? And when the staff starts complainging about the constant pseudo-suffering and non-death, management responds by making them even more humanoid and sympathetic. Not very smart for such a brilliant organization. On the other hand, it does fit in very well with their tendency to rush into exciting projects without really thinking them through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I question that assumption.
Every industry has workplace deaths. Every major construction project has at least a few lethal accidents. Every company applies the "Fight Club" recall logic, and I would argue that is morally equivalent to killing. Odds are that every product warning label you've ever read is the result of at least one fatality. Factor in the world's police, militaries, security contractors, etc. with job description that include at least occasional killing. Religious and para-religious ideological organizations that either directly promote or are twisted to promote the killing of this sub-group or that…

Without hard data, we can't say for sure, but I'd say the lethality of human organizations is closer to 50/50 than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article makes far too much use of the word unknown for my tastes- unknown liquid, unknown means, unknown process. Might as well redact it- or far better, put something interesting in place of all that ignorance. Further, the attached documentation just doesn't do it for me- it veers into cliche with an experiment gone wrong, clones begging for death, the scientist angrily regretting their involvement, the portal reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's just as clear when they're divided up as SCP-1610-A through SCP-1610-D.

No, they will not be. That's the point.

According to the Tag Guide's definition, no, they are neither sentient nor sapient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as such, instances have been divided up into subgroups by symbol, designated as SCP-1610-A through SCP-1610-D,

Shouldn't that be SCP-1610-1-A through SCP-1610-1-D?

It will then create six instances of SCP-1610-1 that are identical to the human immersed.

If the copies are identical to the immersed subjects, then they aren't going to be one of the As through Ds, are they?

All subjects are anatomically identical to humans, but exhibit no signs of sentience

The Cs and Ds would have to be sentient, or they couldn't interact with anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think keeping the original alive so he or she could witness themselves being killed over and over makes this more blood chilling. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was ready to downvote until I saw the addendum.
Good job I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As long as they have been well preserved, yes, you can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of attached to the vocalizations as well. Nice, clear failure messages are wonderful.

My own view of PL is that they were scarily competent, but had a tendency to be short sighted around the edges of things which could cause things to not work out as intended. The vocalizations work into that.

Complete side note, but can you insert dead bodies into the fluid and get clones back? I think it was implied, but it's not spelled out in the article so I thought I would ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me a lot like something Aperture Science would do.

"Cave Johnson here. Just wanted to remind you that Aperture Science is not affiliated with Prometheus Labs nor are we affiliated with their test-subject cloning project, even though we thought up the idea first. In retrospect, we should have filed that patent when we had the chance, but that's water under the bridge. Also, I would like to remind everyone that bring-your-daughter to work day is tomorrow, and participation is mandatory. If you don't have a daughter, well, go adopt one or borrow somebody elses'. See what I care.

This is Cave Johnson. We're done here."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing feels a little over-the-top, and I'm not sure if I can get behind the term "Euclid-class liquid receptacle". How do you design a liquid receptacle so it'll work with any Euclid-class SCP?

On the other hand, this does accomplish the concept. It's not bad, but not good enough for me to upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the process, I believe.

As for the vocalizations…I feel personally attached to those, so unless more people complain about them, I'll probably leave them in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd certainly make it harmless. That addresses the issue, and remedies the worst of the OOC-ness of Prometheus.

I might remove the vocalizations as well. It's so obviously a bad idea that the fact that Prometheus didn't figure that out arguably seems somewhat off. The creepiness of experimenting on something that looks just like you would probably be enough to carry the article without the vocalizations. Could be wrong, though; that one's up to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. How should I restructure it so that it's a stronger point? Have the process be completely harmless?

And I didn't mean to paint PL in that light in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but that's what we feel is a weakness in the article. Plus, it's a bit of a red herring. Even if it is really volunteer only, a reader will think of Prometheus extracting false consent and other dastardly and out of character things, which just distract from the point of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing is, the originals really don't live after the process. See my responses to anqxyr higher up in the thread. People volunteering for the process know that they're not coming back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree with this. There's a big difference between exposing human looking things to dangerous situations and actually killing real people. And there is more interesting stuff you can do with the process being basically harmless to the individual it happens to. I'm a firm believer that an item should be only as dangerous as it absolutely must be.

This still has the problem of the dubious clinical value of the products of this. They're not really human, and they are inherently anomalous, so it may be a mistake to generalize from these to the human case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for understanding this. This article isn't about companies killing humans because, objectively, the Z-Subjects aren't human, if you ask some of the higher up researchers. And the people who were used as the templates for the Z-Subjects were not just forcibly taken from the street; they willingly donated their bodies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. If the homunculi really aren't sentient or sapient, they're perfect for medical research, and probably much more efficient than starting with animal testing before moving onto human trials. The method of creation isn't even much of a problem, because I could easily see a terminally ill person volunteering to undergo the process. To be sure, you would need homunculi of both genders and a wide range of apparent ages, but it's still workable, especially if you found out a way to make more than six from one person. The issue comes when people stop volunteering because the result goes against every human instinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I look at it as I do animal testing. I'm certain PL was doing animal testing, and anything done to non-sentient clones wouldn't really be any different than "regular" animal testing.

The thing I find stretching how I view PL is that the originals die while being cloned. That seems unnecessarily grimdark. (Also, the originals dieing combined with "take your daughter to work day" makes it… a bit lolprometheus.)

Especially when the person living through the copying is actually even more horrific. I mean, it's one thing to watch something that looks human get killed repeatedly. Now imagine a long day of dealing with that, and going home to look the original in the eye. (or perhaps the horror of being the original and watching yourself asking to be disposed of.)

If that one bit could be dropped, I think it'd make it match PL a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in danger of (improperly) going into a rant outlining how often companies willfully endanger people, but instead I'll just bring up TGN1412 as the appropriate counter example. Seemed perfectly safe during animal testing, but human trials were a complete disaster.

If they had some ready made human clones to throw at it, then people wouldn't have been hurt by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, but we have similar examples of companies doing cutting-edge research that may as well be delving a bit into the paranormal. And I'm pretty sure Craig Venter doesn't kill people for science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, the overwhelming majority of human organizations aren't doing the things Prometheus Labs are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The overwhelming majority of human organizations get by without killing anyone. This is grimdark for the sake of grimdark and I loathe that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could sort of feel my disgust growing along with that of the researchers in the recovered notes, so you certainly succeeded on that front. UV'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. It felt like it had potential, but it didn't really do it for me. Reluctant downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to get behind this, but the dialogue in the addendum is awkward and un believable at points… There's not enough build-up to give me a convincing reason the researcher would actually go through with this to begin with, especially PL researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They act in the exact same way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's addressed for 1610-A through -D. It's never made clear what Foundation-made instances of 1601-1 behave like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I'm okay with this being PL.

The Factory can force a uniform behavior through supernatural means. Wondertainment can either do the same, or is a single person in the first place. But PL was a human organization. It had branches. We don't know how independent those branches were. There are amoral or misguided people everywhere, especially in the Foundationverse. I would be more surprised if nothing like this ever happened to them.

Also, the cruel part is debatable. If these homunculi are indeed not sentient, then what was done not only wasn't cruel, but was even preferable to animal testing. You can argue that the personnel's behavior was caused by human tendency to anthropomorphise non-living object, and when that object looks like a human, especially someone they knew, it becomes increasingly hard to remain detached.

Well, they killed five humans to create the homunculi. That was ethically not good. But the process is presumably painless, and five people is not that big of a death toll. The rest is in the grey area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe that's addressed in the article itself.

:U In my world everyone is short okay But yeah, I'll change that.

One of the head researchers. Certainly not the only one, no. Improvements needed to be made to the Z-Subjects, and, well, volunteers were becoming more and more scarce. As for when the Cs and Ds were made, I actually believed they were made at roughly the same time, albeit somewhat separated by a few days to a week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, not sentience, but behavior then. What do they say when killed? How fast they regenerate? Can they tend their wounds?

Regarding the tone. here's an example:

If the damage done would be non-lethal to a human of similar size, then it is repaired at a rapid rate.

I'd word it something like

Damage that would be non-lethal to a normal human of similar size is repaired at a rapid rate.

I suppose there is nothing explicitly wrong with you version. It just seems odd to me for some reason.

Also, something I completely forgot to comment on the first time: why are they all so fucking short? I thought that they were anatomically identical to their originals. You should probably add ~0.3 meters to their height.

If the original remains brain-dead, the fact that 1610-C is made from the head researcher is a bit confusing. Seeing as Cs and Ds are both marked, and Ds were made later, the research was still ongoing at the time Cs were created. If so, who lead the research? I'd imagine that when researchers kill themselves or desert, and then the head researcher trows himself in the vat, they'd just stop the whole thing. Did PL send someone else to lead the project?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would it be better if I just changed it to a different non-GoI institution?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This…. no. Just no.

The concept is workable. I do not like this as a Prometheus Labs related article. That's just not their thing; Prometheus is not amoral, they have never been shown being this cruel, and the tone just completely off for what I feel is appropriate for that GoI. Sorry, but I have to downvote this.

Edit: To clarify, as a significant contributor to Prometheus Labs material, I hate this article because the theme of Prometheus Labs was always that of a commercial corporation really trying its best to do good things for humanity through honest scientific research and development. Throwing in human sacrifice, willing or not, goes against everything they stand for in my opinion, and I despise that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. They manifest within the liquid itself. It isn't an instantaneous process; when one finishes, it can be taken out of the whatever receptacle SCP-XXXX-2 is being held in to make room for more copies, if necessary. Six is just the number it makes1
  2. The original doesn't change. It's useless after it goes through the SCP-1610 process.
  3. According to the Tag Guide's definition of sentient, they aren't sentient at all.

I suppose there isn't. I'll merge the two.

How so? Can you point out some examples of this?

Footnotes
1. Once the liquid starts replicating the first copy, it will make exactly six copies, even if the original is removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I fixed the things you pointed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh good lord.

If a human is immersed, then the liquid will render the subject comatose and disable higher brain functions through an unknown means. It will then create six instances of SCP-1610-1 that are identical to the human immersed.

  1. How, exactly, does that happen? Are all the copies crammed into the tube with 1610-2? Wouldn't that severely damage both them and the original? Or do they just use a bigger tank?
  2. After the copies are created, what happens to the original and his brain functions?
  3. The created copies, where do they fall on the sentience scale? Are they all like 1610-A, 1610-D, or do they vary, or what?

Is there any need to separate the tables into two? It seems to me they would look nicely as one table.

The tone is… odd. Not exactly unclinical, but… odd. I can't quite put my finger on it.

Upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1610-1's anomalous properties manifest when an instance of SCP-1610 experiences physical damage

What's an instance of SCP-1610? SCP-1610-1? SCP-1610-2? Both? Idunno? Am I just loopy due to lack of sleep?

it will be "healed" almost instantaneously

I don't find these quotes necessary.

Mr. Masterson was previously confirmed to be head researcher

I THOUGHT SO!

+1

This is a hook done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear, I proofread this like twice and didn't catch any of these ._.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and more encouraging voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. Just a couple things I wanted to note:

All of you are sick people, and I wish I could take you all with you.

Was this intentional?

Well, that's one batch of clones wasted and one "Bring Your Daughter to Work" day that shan't be forgotten

Nothing wrong here, but it reminds me of Aperture Science. In a more fucked up way, anyway.

+1, keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only in the case where they had to incinerate the batch of little girls because no one wanted to test on them. In this case, it was one of their head researchers/friends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Hesitant upvote. Something about the writing rubs me the wrong way, and I'm left with some questions about how it works (not the good kind). It feels like it was supposed to inspire curiosity but couldn't quit manage it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that we got our fucking [REDACTED] dying in front of us every day.

Is that their children? ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I liked it. One thing:

Maybe a new model is need, and more encouraging voice.

Not sure if intentional or Russian.
